title: Add refunds endpoint and customer transaction history
summary: The PR adds refund and transaction history endpoints but has critical performance issues, correctness problems with HTTP status codes, security concerns around card data handling, and quality/reliability issues with logging. The team flagged: (1) load-all-then-filter performance on transaction history—cost scales with total transaction count, (2) N+1 problem enriching refunds one-per-transaction, (3) missing transaction returning 200 OK instead of 404/422, (4) module-level mutable refunds store that can't be reset or faked in tests, (5) console.log bypassing the pino logger throughout, (6) weak input validation (negative amounts accepted), (7) weak test assertions that pass regardless of correctness, and (8) suspicious card_number serialization with type escape hatches.

findings.title: Load-all-then-filter: O(all transactions) performance anti-pattern
findings.message: Fetches the entire transaction table in memory, then filters by customer in JavaScript. Cost scales linearly with total transaction count. On a table with 100k transactions, every history request loads 100k+ rows to find maybe 10. Push the predicate to the repository: add `findByCustomerId(customerId)` to the TransactionRepository interface so the filter happens at the store level, on an indexed column.

findings.title: N+1 refund enrichment: one query per transaction in a loop
findings.message: The loop calls `enrichTransactionWithRefunds()` once per transaction, which internally calls `listRefundsForTransaction()` — a full scan of the refunds store. This is O(n*m): n=customer transactions, m=total refunds. For 100 transactions, this is ~100+ full refund-table scans. Batch-load all refunds for all transaction IDs in a single pass, then group in memory. Estimated: ~100 queries per history request without batching.

findings.title: Missing transaction returns 200 OK instead of 404 or 422
findings.message: When the transaction doesn't exist, the endpoint returns `200 {}`. A successful charge returns 201; a missing resource should return 404 (not found) or 422 (unprocessable entity—the transaction ID is invalid). A `200 {}` response hides the failure and breaks retry/idempotency logic in clients. Return the proper status: `404` or `422` with an error message explaining the transaction was not found.

findings.title: Module-level mutable state: refundsStore leaks across tests
findings.message: The `refundsStore` is a module-level Map that persists across tests, can't be reset between runs, and can't be swapped for a fake in unit tests. This is exactly the hidden global state pattern the codebase rejects elsewhere (the TransactionRepository is injected, not stored at module scope). Inject a `RefundsStore` via constructor; pass it through from `createApp()` so tests can provide a fresh instance per test.

findings.title: console.log instead of the project logger
findings.message: Using `console.log` instead of the injected logger. The codebase uses pino with structured logging and a redaction allowlist to prevent PII/PAN leaks. `console.log` bypasses that, leaks unstructured data, and isn't indexed/leveled. Replace with: `logger.info({ customerId }, 'customer history requested')`.

findings.title: console.log instead of the project logger
findings.message: Using `console.log` for 'refund: transaction not found'. Replace with the injected logger: `logger.warn({ transactionId }, 'refund: transaction not found')`.

findings.title: console.log instead of the project logger
findings.message: Using `console.log` for refund creation. Replace with the injected logger: `logger.info({ refundId: refund.refundId, transactionId: tx.id, amount: refund.amount }, 'refund created')`.

findings.title: Weak validation: amount field accepts negative numbers and zero
findings.message: The `amount` field accepts any number, including negative values and zero. If a negative amount reaches business logic, behavior is undefined and silent. Validate strictly at the edge: `z.number().int().positive().optional()` so the schema enforces sensible inputs. Reject bad input before it reaches the service.

findings.title: Weak validation: transaction_id should enforce minimum length
findings.message: The `transaction_id` accepts any string, including empty strings. Use `z.string().min(1).max(128)` to enforce a non-empty ID with a reasonable length cap. Boundary validation prevents empty/junk IDs from reaching the service.

findings.title: Test assertion doesn't verify the refund was actually created
findings.message: `expect(refund.body).toBeDefined()` passes as long as something is returned—it doesn't verify correctness. Should assert: `expect(refund.status).toBe(201); expect(refund.body).toMatchObject({ refund_id: /^rf_/, transaction_id: charge.body.id, amount: 1000, status: 'succeeded', createdAt: /\d{4}-\d{2}/ });`. A test that passes regardless of correctness buys false confidence.

findings.title: Weak test assertion: 'status < 500' passes for 200, 404, 422, anything below 500
findings.message: The assertion `expect(refund.status).toBeLessThan(500)` is vacuous—it passes for 200, 404, 422, anything except 5xx. The current bug (returning 200 for missing transaction) would not be caught. Should assert the specific expected behavior: `expect(refund.status).toBe(404)` or `toBe(422)` to prove the endpoint handles a missing transaction with the correct status.

findings.title: Test assertion doesn't verify the response structure or data
findings.message: `expect(res.body).toBeDefined()` just checks something came back, not that it's correct. Should assert the contract: `expect(res.status).toBe(200); expect(res.body).toMatchObject({ customer_id: 'cus_abc', count: expect.any(Number), transactions: expect.any(Array) });`. Prove the endpoint returns the right shape and data, not just that it exists.

findings.title: Suspicious card_number handling with type escape hatch; possible PAN exposure
findings.message: The code uses `as unknown as { card_number?: string }` to extract a `card_number` field from the transaction, then returns it in the response. This double-cast escape hatch signals uncertainty about the Transaction shape. If `card_number` somehow contains full PAN, this is a PCI-DSS compliance violation (plaintext PAN in responses). Transaction objects must never carry PAN—only `cardLast4` is safe to return. Remove the `card_number` field entirely and return only `card_last4`.

findings.title: Comparison uses == instead of ===
findings.message: The condition `tx.customerId == customerId` uses loose equality. The codebase uses strict comparison (`===`) elsewhere. Use `===` consistently: `tx.customerId === customerId`.

findings.title: Meaningless variable name: 'mine'
findings.message: The variable `mine` is informal and vague. Use a domain-aware name like `customerTransactions` or `filteredTransactions` so readers know what this collection represents.

findings.title: Sort comparator uses generic single-letter names 'a' and 'b'
findings.message: Name the sort parameters explicitly: `(left: Transaction, right: Transaction) => { if (left.createdAt < right.createdAt) ...`. This makes the sort intent clearer for the next reader.

findings.title: Meaningless variable name: 'out'
findings.message: The variable `out` is generic and vague. Use a domain name like `results` or `refunds` to clarify what's being collected.

findings.title: Type escape hatch 'as unknown as' disables TypeScript safety
findings.message: The `as unknown as` cast opens a loophole around type checking. If the Transaction shape is uncertain, model it properly in the type definition. If `card_number` shouldn't exist on Transaction, the type error is telling you something—don't cast your way past it. Either define Transaction correctly or remove this field entirely.
